reference: GATE CS — Computer Networks / Transport Layer

formulas.name: Bandwidth-Delay Product
formulas.plain: Window (bits) needed to keep a pipe full = bandwidth x round-trip time
formulas.whenToUse: Sizing the window for 100% link utilization.
formulas.trap: If window < BDP, throughput is window/RTT, not bandwidth.

formulas.name: TCP Throughput
formulas.plain: Steady-state throughput approx = window size / RTT (when window-limited)

terms.means: usable window (min of cwnd and rwnd)

formulas.trap: Effective window = min(congestion window, receiver advertised window).

formulas.name: Sequence-Number Wraparound
formulas.plain: TCP's 32-bit byte sequence number wraps after 2^32 bytes; fast links wrap quickly
formulas.whenToUse: Reasoning about seq-number reuse on high-speed links.
formulas.trap: TCP sequence numbers count BYTES, not segments.

formulas.name: TCP vs UDP Header
formulas.plain: TCP header 20 bytes (up to 60 with options); UDP header fixed 8 bytes
formulas.trap: UDP is unreliable/connectionless; TCP adds reliability, ordering, flow & congestion control.

formulas.name: Maximum Segment Size
formulas.plain: MSS = MTU - IP header - TCP header (typically MTU - 40 bytes)
formulas.whenToUse: Sizing TCP segments to avoid IP fragmentation.
formulas.trap: MSS is payload only (excludes headers); negotiated in the SYN.

formulas.name: Connection 4-Tuple
formulas.plain: A TCP connection is uniquely identified by the 4-tuple of source/dest IP and port
formulas.trap: One listening server port can host many connections, each distinguished by the client side of the tuple.

formulas.name: Three-Way Handshake
formulas.plain: TCP setup takes 3 segments (1.5 RTT before data); teardown uses FIN/ACK (4-way)
formulas.trap: Data can piggyback on the third (ACK) segment; setup adds ~1 RTT before useful data.
